description: Deep Q&A conversation with high-value question-answer pairs

input.conversation:
Student: What's the difference between TCP and UDP?
AI: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is connection-oriented, providing reliable, ordered delivery with error checking. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is connectionless, offering faster but unreliable delivery.
Student: So when would I use UDP over TCP?
AI: UDP is preferred for real-time applications like video streaming, online gaming, and VoIP where speed matters more than perfect reliability. Losing a few packets is acceptable - you'd rather see a slightly glitchy video than a paused one.
Student: That makes sense! So basically TCP = reliability, UDP = speed?
AI: That's a good simplification, but there's more nuance. TCP also has flow control and congestion control mechanisms. And modern protocols like QUIC (used by HTTP/3) actually build reliability on top of UDP to get the best of both worlds.
Student: Wait, HTTP/3 uses UDP? That's surprising!
input.subject: Computer Networking

tips.content: QUIC (HTTP/3) builds reliability on top of UDP to combine advantages of both TCP and UDP
tips.evidence: AI: modern protocols like QUIC (used by HTTP/3) actually build reliability on top of UDP

tips.content: TCP = connection-oriented, reliable, ordered; UDP = connectionless, fast, unreliable
tips.evidence: Student: So basically TCP = reliability, UDP = speed?
